text: The surgeon identifies adhesions involving the sigmoid colon and infundibulopelvic ligament. The ureter is visualized, and the pararectal space is opened up to the level of the Denonvilliers' fascia. The peritoneum is opened by lateralizing the ureter towards the pouch of Douglas. The rectum is separated from the posterior margins of the vagina and pushed away, with fat dissected away from the posterior vaginal aspect up to the external sphincter.

The surgeon begins bladder dissection by pushing the bladder down to achieve a clear margin from the vaginal vault. The dissection proceeds laterally due to dense adhesions between the bladder and the anterior vaginal wall. Traction is applied to the opened vaginal vault, allowing the surgeon to separate the vagina from the bladder, ideally extending to the level of the trigone.

The surgeon opens the Yakubish space to push the ureter away, focusing on the parametrectomy dissection. Dense fibrosis is visible at the level of the external iliac vein, which is released using a laparoscopic shearer. Electrocoagulation is applied to safely dissect around the great vessels.

The surgeon releases fibrosis and identifies the obliterated umbilical artery with the uterine artery originating from it, marking the beginning of the vascular parametrium. Dissection continues by opening the ureteric tunnel, using scissors for precise cutting.

The surgeon completes dissection up to the entry point of the ureteric tunnel. The parametrium is lifted and flipped medially, tracing the obliterated umbilical upwards. Fibrosis from the previous surgery is released, and the paravesical space is opened. Care is taken to avoid the obturator nerve during gentle dissection.

The surgeon lifts the parametrium by coagulating structures except for the uterine artery, preserving the branch going into the ureter. The uterine artery is then coagulated, freeing the parametrium significantly. The deep uterine vein is identified to preserve the nerve plexus.

The surgeon identifies the deep uterine vein embedded within the surrounding adipose tissue in an obese patient. Using a laparoscopic shearer, the surgeon carefully dissects the ureteric tunnel, ensuring the ureter is safely lateralized without thermal injury. The dissection frees significant tissue margins, minimizing the risk to the ureter.

The surgeon further frees the ureter from the ureteric tunnel, coagulating the visible uterine vein. The dissection progresses into anterior and cranial planes to free the parametrium, which consists of fibro-fatty tissue. The ureter is mobilized laterally to ensure free margins at the vaginal vault. The venous structure in the parametrium is coagulated above the ureter, minimizing the risk of thermal injury. The ureter is pushed down away from the vault, creating a safe distance from the vagina and the line of coagulation. The ureter is seen entering the urinary bladder, and dissection mobilizes it from the lateral wall of the vagina.
